title: The Fight Against Colony Collapse Disorder Started 19 Years Ago Today
gist: Marking the 19th anniversary of the USDA's 2007 action plan against colony collapse disorder, the piece recalls how the mysterious illness killed 30-90% of some U.S. honeybee hives in winter 2006-2007, threatening pollination for over 130 food crops that make up a third of the American diet.

insights.claim: Colony collapse disorder killed between 30 and 90 percent of some U.S. honeybee hives during the winter of 2006-2007, prompting a government emergency response.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: A historical severity benchmark for pollinator-collapse events, useful context for assessing current hive-health data.

insights.claim: Honeybees, including species imported from Europe, pollinate more than 130 food crops in the U.S., accounting for roughly one third of the American food supply.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of agricultural exposure to pollinator health, relevant to ag-sector risk assessment.

insights.claim: The USDA announced a coordinated research action plan in July 2007 to study and combat colony collapse disorder, after officials warned beekeepers could face a serious problem the following year.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Establishes the origin point of the ongoing federal CCD research effort now approaching its 20th year.
insights.quote: There were enough honeybees to provide pollination for U.S. agriculture this year, but beekeepers could face a serious problem next year and beyond.
